questions.specification: A region once containing a complex wetland ecosystem has been drained and urbanized. Which of the following consequences is least likely to occur?

options.content: Decreased groundwater recharge
options.markscheme: Draining and urbanizing a wetland typically reduces infiltration because impervious surfaces increase runoff, so decreased (not increased) groundwater recharge is likely.

options.content: Reduction in native bird populations
options.markscheme: Wetland drainage and urbanization remove habitat and food sources for wetland-dependent birds, so reduced native bird populations are likely.

options.content: Loss of breeding sites for amphibians
options.markscheme: Amphibians often require wetland habitats for breeding; drainage and development remove these sites, so this consequence is likely.

options.content: Lower water quality due to runoff
options.markscheme: Urbanization typically increases surface runoff carrying pollutants (e.g., oils, nutrients, sediments) into waterways, lowering water quality; this is likely.

questions.specification:
Ecologists monitored the development of an ecosystem on a newly formed volcanic island over a period of 150 years. The study tracked changes in biomass and energy flow as the community underwent primary succession.

| Stage | Years since formation | Total Biomass ($kg\ m^{-2}$) | Gross Primary Productivity ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) | Respiration ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pioneer | 10 | 0.2 | 150 | 90 |
| Intermediate | 50 | 4.5 | 800 | 450 |
| Climax | 150 | 18.2 | 1200 | 1150 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is explicitly about primary succession on a newly formed volcanic island, tracking biomass changes across pioneer/intermediate/climax stages and asking about how conditions change during succession.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter > C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Calculating NPP from GPP and respiration and discussing changing energy storage as climax is approached directly tests primary production/biomass accumulation concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: References to the ecosystem approaching a climax community and comparison of energy flow/respiration in the climax stage align with climax community characteristics.

parts.content: State the relationship between time and total biomass during this process of primary succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Total biomass increases with time during succession (positive correlation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is lowest in the pioneer stage and highest in the climax stage (increasing trend across stages). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the net primary productivity (NPP) for the intermediate stage. Then discuss how energy storage (biomass accumulation/NPP) changes from the intermediate stage to the climax community.
parts.markscheme:
- NPP calculation: $\text{NPP} = \text{GPP} - R = 800 - 450 = 350\ g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct units stated for NPP: $g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From intermediate to climax, respiration increases substantially (higher community maintenance costs). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the climax stage, $\text{GPP} \approx R$ (e.g. $1200$ vs $1150$), so NPP is low and little additional energy is stored as new biomass. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is still high in the climax community because it has accumulated over time, even though net biomass gain per year is small. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how pioneer species, such as lichens and mosses, alter the abiotic environment to allow for the establishment of later successional stages.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical and/or chemical weathering: pioneer organisms break down bare rock to produce smaller particles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Organic matter/humus addition: death and decomposition of pioneers adds organic material, improving water retention and soil structure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Nutrient enrichment: some pioneers (e.g. cyanobacteria in lichens) fix nitrogen, increasing soil fertility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award any two of the above points. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content:
The graph shows the change in population size of *Daphnia* (water fleas) introduced into a closed freshwater tank over a period of 50 days.

![Daphnia Population Growth Curve](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-19e520ef-bad2-4f4e-beb0-b4fe682b8dd3-1771929956947/c0e6dd8c-0a6d-4451-bb1f-bba1af2e6bb9.jpeg)

Describe and explain the population growth pattern shown in the graph. <marks>5 marks</marks>
parts.markscheme:
- exponential phase (early phase): rapid growth where natality exceeds mortality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- exponential growth occurs because resources (food/space) are not yet limiting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- transitional phase (middle phase): growth rate begins to slow down <marks>1 mark</marks>
- slowing growth due to increased competition for food / accumulation of metabolic waste / density-dependent limiting factors <marks>1 mark</marks>
- plateau phase (late phase): population size levels off / reaches carrying capacity/$K$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- at the plateau: natality + immigration equals mortality + emigration / population is stable <marks>1 mark</marks>
(Maximum 5 marks)

questions.specification:
The table shows biochemical characteristics of three domains of life.

| Feature | Domain I | Domain II | Domain III |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Histones | Absent | Present | Present in some species |
| Introns | Rare or absent | Frequent | Present in some genes |
| Peptidoglycan in the cell wall | Present | Absent | Absent |

Which domains contain organisms that are classified as prokaryotes?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.9 Classification of all organisms using evidence from rRNA base sequences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.9 Classification of all organisms using evidence from rRNA base sequences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses a comparative table of biochemical features (histones, introns, peptidoglycan) to distinguish between the three domains of life (Bacteria, Archaea, Eukaryota), which is the core content of the three-domain classification system.

options.content: I and II
options.markscheme: This is incorrect because Domain II possesses histones and frequent introns, identifying it as Eukaryota, which are eukaryotes rather than prokaryotes.

options.content: I and III
options.markscheme: This is correct. Domain I represents Bacteria (peptidoglycan present, histones absent) and Domain III represents Archaea (no peptidoglycan, some histones/introns). Both Bacteria and Archaea are prokaryotic domains.

options.content: II and III
options.markscheme: This is incorrect because Domain II is Eukaryota, which contains only eukaryotic organisms. While Domain III (Archaea) is prokaryotic, the pair together is not correct.

options.content: I only
options.markscheme: This is incorrect because it excludes Domain III. Archaea (Domain III) are also prokaryotes, as they lack a nuclear envelope despite having some biochemical similarities to eukaryotes.

The table provides information about two greenhouse gases found in the Earth's atmosphere.

| Greenhouse Gas | Relative warming potential (100-year, $CO_2 = 1$) | Concentration in the atmosphere (ppm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Methane ($CH_4$) | 28 | 1.9 |
| Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) | 1 | 412.0 |

Which statement best explains why carbon dioxide has a larger overall effect on global warming than methane?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Compares methane and carbon dioxide using relative warming potential and atmospheric concentration to explain overall impact on global warming—core to anthropogenic causes of climate change and greenhouse gases.

options.content: Its concentration in the atmosphere is significantly higher.
options.markscheme: Correct. Despite lower relative warming potential per molecule, $CO_2$ is far more abundant (412.0 ppm vs 1.9 ppm), giving a larger overall effect.

options.content: It is more efficient at absorbing long-wave radiation per molecule.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The table indicates methane has the higher relative warming potential per molecule (28 vs 1).

options.content: It is the only greenhouse gas that persists in the atmosphere for more than a year.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is not supported by the data, and methane can persist for more than a year.

options.content: It reflects a larger proportion of incoming short-wave solar radiation.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Greenhouse gases warm mainly by absorbing outgoing long-wave (infrared) radiation, not by reflecting short-wave radiation.

questions.specification: The greenhouse effect is influenced by the specific properties of various atmospheric gases. In addition to infrared absorption, which factor determines a gas’s overall contribution to global warming?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.3 Climate change > D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what determines a greenhouse gas’s overall contribution to global warming besides IR absorption—this aligns with understanding drivers of climate change, including the role of atmospheric concentrations of greenhouse gases.

options.content: The density of the gas relative to the surrounding air
options.markscheme: The density of a gas determines its buoyancy or layering in the atmosphere, but it does not dictate its ability to trap heat or contribute to the greenhouse effect.

options.content: The concentration of the gas in the atmosphere
options.markscheme: The total impact of a greenhouse gas depends on two main factors: its ability to absorb long-wave (infrared) radiation and its abundance/concentration in the atmosphere.

options.content: The ability of the gas to reflect incoming short-wave radiation
options.markscheme: Reflecting short-wave radiation (albedo) would actually have a cooling effect; greenhouse gases work by absorbing long-wave radiation emitted from the Earth's surface.

questions.specification:
A conservation study measured the population size of an endangered mammal species in a fragmented habitat over five different regions. The table below shows the data collected:

| Region | Habitat Size (km²) | Mammal Population |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| A      | 500               | 250              |
| B      | 300               | 160              |
| C      | 150               | 85               |
| D      | 75                | 40               |
| E      | 30                | 12               |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.2.4 Causes of ecosystem loss
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario is explicitly about fragmented habitat and its impact on an endangered species; this fits ecosystem/habitat loss as a driver affecting populations.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.1.1 Populations

parts.content: Identify the relationship between habitat size and mammal population. Suggest one reason for this trend.
parts.markscheme:
- Larger habitats support higher populations; smaller habitats have fewer mammals.
- Example: Region A (500 km²) has 250 mammals, while Region E (30 km²) has only 12 mammals.
- Small habitats have fewer resources, more competition, and greater predation risks.

<marks> Total 2 marks </marks> : <marks> 1 mark </marks>  identifying the trend, <marks> 1 mark </marks>  explaining resource availability and population decline

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content:
To answer this question, we need to do two things:
1. Observe the data to identify the **relationship** (the trend) between habitat size and population.
2. Use **ecological concepts** to explain why that trend exists.

This aligns with **Topic C4.1: Populations and communities**, where we study how resources and environment affect population size.

steps.title: Analyze data trends
steps.content:
By looking at the table, we can see a clear pattern: as the habitat size decreases, the mammal population also decreases. Conversely, as the habitat size increases, the population size increases.

This is a **positive relationship** (or positive correlation). In biology, we often find that the physical size of an environment directly limits the number of organisms that can live there.

highlights.text: Identify the relationship between habitat size and mammal population

steps.title: Provide evidence
steps.content:
To secure the first mark, it's best to provide a specific example from the data to illustrate the trend. 

Compare the two extremes:
*   **Region A** has the largest habitat ($500\text{ km}^2$) and the highest population ($250$).
*   **Region E** has the smallest habitat ($30\text{ km}^2$) and the lowest population ($12$).

This evidence confirms the positive relationship.

highlights.text: Example: Region A (500 km²) has 250 mammals, while Region E (30 km²) has only 12 mammals.

steps.title: Ecological explanation
steps.content:
The reason for this trend is linked to **carrying capacity** ($K$) and **resource availability**. 

Larger habitats typically provide more essential resources such as food, water, and nesting sites. Smaller habitats have fewer of these resources, which leads to:
*   Increased **interspecific and intraspecific competition**.
*   Lower carrying capacity, meaning the environment can only support a smaller number of individuals.
*   Potential issues like higher predation risk or lack of mates due to fragmentation.

highlights.text: Carrying capacity and competition for limited resources

steps.title: Final summary
steps.content:
To gain the full 2 marks, your answer should look like this:

*   **Trend:** There is a positive relationship where larger habitats support larger mammal populations (e.g., Region A vs Region E).
*   **Reason:** Smaller habitats have fewer resources (food/water), which limits the carrying capacity and increases competition for what is available.

steps.title: Analyze the data table
steps.content:
To identify the relationship, we look at the extreme values in the data. By comparing the largest and smallest regions, we can see a clear trend.

*   **Region A** (the largest) has a habitat size of $500\text{ km}^2$ and supports $250$ mammals.
*   **Region E** (the smallest) has a habitat size of $30\text{ km}^2$ and supports only $12$ mammals.

As the habitat size decreases, the population of mammals also decreases, indicating a **positive correlation** between habitat size and population size.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the largest population to the smallest population to see the magnitude of the difference.

highlights.text: Region A
Region E

steps.title: Evaluate population density
steps.content:
Using a calculator to find the population density (individuals per $\text{km}^2$), we can see that smaller habitats may struggle to support mammals as efficiently as larger ones.

$$\text{Density}_A = \frac{250}{500} = 0.50\text{ mammals/km}^2$$
$$\text{Density}_E = \frac{12}{30} = 0.40\text{ mammals/km}^2$$

The lower density in the smallest region suggests that smaller fragments are not just smaller, but potentially less suitable for the species.

gdc.instructions: Divide the population by the habitat size for Regions A and E.

calculator.description: Calculate population density for Region A and Region E.

steps.title: Suggest an ecological reason
steps.content:
One primary reason for this trend is **resource availability** and the **carrying capacity** of the habitat. 

Larger habitats provide more food, water, and nesting sites, allowing for a larger carrying capacity (covered in **Topic C4.1: Populations and communities**). Conversely, small fragments suffer from **habitat fragmentation** and **edge effects**, where the perimeter of the habitat is exposed to outside disturbances (like noise or predators), reducing the high-quality interior space available for the mammals to survive.

steps.title: Final answer formulation
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your answer includes both the data-backed trend and a biological explanation:

1.  **Trend:** Larger habitats support higher mammal populations. For example, Region A ($500\text{ km}^2$) has $250$ mammals, while Region E ($30\text{ km}^2$) has only $12$.
2.  **Reason:** Smaller habitats have fewer available resources (food/space) and may be more affected by edge effects, reducing the population they can support.

highlights.text: Larger habitats support higher populations; smaller habitats have fewer mammals.
Small habitats have fewer resources, more competition, and greater predation risks.

parts.content: Describe how habitat corridors could help in the conservation of this endangered species.
parts.markscheme:
- Habitat corridors are connections between fragmented habitats that allow safe movement of animals.
- This increases genetic diversity, reduces inbreeding, and enables access to food and mates.
- It helps maintain stable populations and prevents local extinctions.

<marks> Total 2 marks </marks> : <marks> 1 mark </marks>  explaining corridors as habitat connections, <marks> 1 mark </marks>  linking them to genetic diversity and survival benefits

steps.title: Define habitat corridors
steps.content: In the **Biological Connectivity Approach**, habitat corridors are defined as physical links or "bridges" of natural habitat that connect otherwise isolated fragments. In our case, these corridors would link regions like A, B, C, D, and E, allowing the endangered mammals to travel safely between them rather than being trapped in a single, small patch.

highlights.text: Habitat corridors are connections between fragmented habitats

steps.title: Facilitate movement and dispersal
steps.content: By providing these physical links, corridors facilitate the movement of individuals. This is crucial for small populations (like the 12 mammals in Region E) that might otherwise run out of resources. Movement allows them to access a wider range of food sources and find suitable nesting or denning sites that might not be available in a single fragment.

steps.title: Increase genetic diversity
steps.content: A major benefit of biological connectivity is the exchange of genes between different subpopulations. When individuals move between regions to find mates, it increases the **genetic diversity** of the species. This helps reduce the risk of inbreeding, which often leads to health issues and lower survival rates in isolated, endangered populations.

highlights.text: increases genetic diversity, reduces inbreeding, and enables access to food and mates

steps.title: Prevent local extinction
steps.content: Finally, these corridors help maintain more stable populations overall. If a local catastrophe occurs in one region (like a disease outbreak in Region D), individuals from other regions can eventually recolonize that area via the corridors. This prevents local extinctions and ensures the long-term survival of the species across the entire landscape.

highlights.text: helps maintain stable populations and prevents local extinctions

steps.title: Define habitat corridors
steps.content:
Habitat corridors are narrow strips or "bridges" of natural habitat that connect isolated patches, such as the fragmented regions **A** through **E** mentioned in the data. By providing a physical connection, they allow animals to move safely between areas that would otherwise be separated by human-developed land or inhospitable terrain.

This concept is a core part of **Topic C4.1: Populations and Communities**, which looks at how populations interact within their environment.

highlights.text: Habitat corridors are connections between fragmented habitats that allow safe movement of animals.

steps.title: Preventing local extinctions
steps.content:
From the **Metapopulation Stability Approach**, we view these regions as a "metapopulation"—a group of separated populations that interact. Small regions, like **Region E** with only $12$ individuals, are at high risk of **local extinction** due to random events or disease.

Corridors help by:
*   **Recolonization**: If the population in Region E dies out, individuals from larger regions (like Region A) can migrate through the corridor to re-establish the population.
*   **The Rescue Effect**: Immigrants from larger patches can supplement declining populations in smaller patches, preventing them from reaching zero.

steps.title: Increase genetic diversity
steps.content:
By allowing movement between fragments, corridors promote **gene flow**. This increases the genetic diversity of the endangered species across all regions. 

When populations are isolated, they often suffer from inbreeding, which weakens their ability to survive environmental changes. Corridors ensure that mates can be found in different regions, maintaining a healthier, more resilient gene pool and ensuring long-term survival.

highlights.text: This increases genetic diversity, reduces inbreeding, and enables access to food and mates.

steps.title: Final summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on an IB exam, ensure you connect the physical structure to the biological outcome:
1.  **Define**: Corridors connect fragmented habitats for safe movement ($1$ mark).
2.  **Benefit**: They allow for recolonization and increased genetic diversity, which prevents local extinctions ($1$ mark).

steps.title: Identify the threat of fragmentation
steps.content: The data provided shows that as habitat size decreases, the mammal population drops significantly—from $250$ in Region A to only $12$ in Region E. Small, isolated populations are highly vulnerable to extinction due to human-made barriers (like roads or fences) that trap them in these fragments.

steps.title: Define habitat corridors
steps.content: A habitat corridor is a strip of natural habitat that acts as a **connection between fragmented habitats**. By linking regions like D and E back to larger areas like A, we recreate a larger, continuous ecosystem.

highlights.text: connections between fragmented habitats

steps.title: Mitigate human-made barriers
steps.content: Following the **Human-Impact Mitigation Approach**, corridors (such as wildlife overpasses or underpasses) provide **safe passage** across human-made barriers like roads. This directly reduces mortality caused by vehicle collisions and allows animals to traverse the landscape without being killed by human activity.

steps.title: Enhance genetic diversity
steps.content: Allowing safe movement enables individuals from different regions to mate. This **increases genetic diversity and reduces inbreeding**, which is critical for the survival of the species in Region E, where only $12$ individuals remain. This gene flow ensures the population remains healthy and can adapt to environmental changes.

highlights.text: increases genetic diversity, reduces inbreeding

steps.title: Final answer for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your description includes two key points:
1. **Corridors connect fragments**: They allow movement between isolated patches.
2. **Survival Benefits**: They allow for access to mates/food, which increases genetic diversity and prevents local extinction.

parts.content: Describe the trend shown in the data and suggest a possible explanation for the observed decline in species richness.
parts.markscheme:
- The number of species decreases over time after deforestation.
- Initially, 125 species were present, but after 10 years, only 45 species remained.
- Deforestation destroys habitats, reducing food availability and shelter, leading to species migration or extinction.

<marks> Total 2 marks </marks> : <marks> 1 mark </marks>  describing the trend, <marks> 1 mark </marks>  explaining habitat loss and its impact on species survival

steps.title: Analyze the quantitative data
steps.content:
To describe the trend effectively in an IB Biology response, you should always reference the specific numerical values provided in the table. 

Looking at the data, we can see a clear **negative correlation** (or downward trend) between the time since deforestation and species richness. 
- At year 0 (the intact forest), there were **125 species**.
- By year 10, the number of species had fallen to **45**.

This represents a significant loss of biodiversity over a relatively short decade.

steps.title: Explain the biological decline
steps.content:
Now, we need to suggest *why* this decline occurs. In IB Biology, particularly when discussing **Topic D4.2 (Sustainability and Change)**, we look at how deforestation alters the physical environment.

Deforestation leads to **habitat destruction**. Trees are not just plants; they are the physical structure of the rainforest. When they are removed, there is a massive **loss of shelter** and nesting sites for various organisms. Furthermore, the removal of the canopy reduces food availability (fruits, leaves, and prey living in the trees), which forces species to either migrate to remaining forest patches or face local extinction.

steps.title: Review the marks awarded
steps.content:
To ensure you get the full 2 marks for this question, check that your answer includes both components of the markscheme:

1.  **Trend Description (1 mark):** You stated that species richness decreases over time and used the specific values from the table ($125 \rightarrow 45$).
2.  **Biological Explanation (1 mark):** You explained that deforestation destroys habitats, leading to a loss of food or shelter.

This quantitative approach is favored by examiners because it shows you can interpret data precisely rather than just making general statements.

highlights.text: 125 species were present, but after 10 years, only 45 species remained
Deforestation destroys habitats, reducing food availability and shelter

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content:
To answer the first part of the question, we need to look at how the **Number of Species Observed** changes as the **Years Since Deforestation** increase. 

Looking at the table, we can see an **inverse correlation**: as the time since deforestation increases, the number of species decreases. This means that the biodiversity of the area is declining over the 10-year period.

highlights.text: Number of Species Observed
Years Since Deforestation

steps.title: Provide data evidence
steps.content:
To get full marks for a "describe" question in IB Biology, it is always a good idea to cite specific values from the data provided.

At the start ($0$ years), the forest was intact with $125$ species. By the end of the study ($10$ years), the number of species had dropped significantly to only $45$.

highlights.text: 0 (Intact Forest)
10 Years

steps.title: Explain the ecological impact
steps.content:
Now we need to explain *why* this decline happens using the **Qualitative Relationship** approach. 

Deforestation removes the primary producers (trees), which are the foundation of the ecosystem. This results in:
1. **Reduction of Ecological Niches**: Trees provide complex 3D structures for nesting, hiding, and hunting. When they are removed, many specialized roles (niches) disappear, and the organisms that filled them can no longer survive there.
2. **Reduced Food Resources**: Many species rely directly on fruits, leaves, or other organisms within the forest for food. Without these resources, species must either migrate to find new habitats or face local extinction.

This aligns with concepts in **Topic B4.2: Ecological Niches** and **Topic D4.2: Sustainability and Change**.

steps.title: Finalize the answer
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, your final answer should clearly state both the trend and the ecological reason:

*   **Trend:** The species richness decreases over time following deforestation (from $125$ to $45$ species over $10$ years).
*   **Explanation:** Deforestation leads to habitat destruction, which reduces available ecological niches and food resources, making it impossible for many species to survive.

parts.content: Explain two conservation strategies that could be used to protect biodiversity in tropical rainforests.
parts.markscheme:
- Protected Areas & National Parks: Legal protection of forests prevents deforestation and preserves biodiversity.
- Reforestation & Habitat Restoration: Planting native trees can rebuild ecosystems and support species recovery.

<marks> Total 2 marks </marks> : <marks> 1 mark </marks>  each valid conservation strategy

steps.title: Understand the Conservation Goal
steps.content: The question asks for strategies to protect biodiversity in tropical rainforests, specifically in the context of the data showing a sharp decline in species richness after deforestation. Using an **In-situ Conservation Approach**, we focus on protecting species within their natural environment to maintain ecological stability.

steps.title: Establish Protected Areas
steps.content:
The first strategy is the establishment of **Protected Areas and National Parks**. By legally safeguarding specific regions of the rainforest, human interference like logging or mining is restricted. This ensures that the "Intact Forest" environment remains stable, preventing the species richness from dropping as seen in the provided table.

$$ \text{Species loss prevention} = \text{Legal protection of habitat} $$

highlights.text: Protected Areas & National Parks: Legal protection of forests prevents deforestation and preserves biodiversity.

steps.title: Implement Habitat Restoration
steps.content:
The second strategy is **Reforestation and Habitat Restoration**. For areas that have already been deforested (like the 10-year mark in the data), planting native tree species helps rebuild the ecosystem. This allows species to return and recolonize the area, reversing the trend of biodiversity loss.

$$ \text{Recovery of biodiversity} \propto \text{Native tree planting} $$

highlights.text: Reforestation & Habitat Restoration: Planting native trees can rebuild ecosystems and support species recovery.

steps.title: Final Explanation for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, you should clearly state two distinct strategies:
1. **Protected Areas:** Creating national parks to prevent future deforestation.
2. **Reforestation:** Actively planting native vegetation to restore damaged habitats and support species recovery.

steps.title: Identify conservation goals
steps.content: To protect biodiversity in tropical rainforests, we focus on **Ecological Restoration**. This involves actively repairing ecosystems that have been degraded or destroyed. Since the data shows a significant drop in species richness over 10 years, our strategies must aim to reverse this trend by rebuilding the habitat structure.

steps.title: Strategy 1: Reforestation programs
steps.content:
One primary strategy is **reforestation**, which involves planting native tree species in deforested areas. This process helps restore the complex vertical structure of the rainforest, providing niches for various organisms and reversing the decline in species richness seen in the data table.

By selecting a diversity of native species, we can accelerate the recovery of the ecosystem and provide the necessary food and shelter for specialized tropical species.

highlights.text: Reforestation & Habitat Restoration: Planting native trees can rebuild ecosystems and support species recovery.

steps.title: Strategy 2: Biological corridors
steps.content:
A second vital strategy is the creation of **biological corridors**. When forests are cleared, the remaining areas become isolated 'islands.' Restoration efforts create strips of habitat that reconnect these fragments.

These corridors allow animals to move between areas to find food and mates, which promotes genetic diversity and prevents local extinctions in small forest patches. This is a key part of **Topic D4.2: Sustainability and Change**, specifically regarding the restoration of natural processes.

steps.title: Review markscheme requirements
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you need to provide two distinct strategies. While our approach focuses on active restoration, the markscheme also recognizes legal protection as a valid strategy.

1. **Reforestation/Habitat Restoration**: Actively planting trees to rebuild the ecosystem (1 mark).
2. **Biological Corridors or Protected Areas**: Either reconnecting fragments or establishing national parks to prevent further loss (1 mark).

highlights.text: 1 mark each valid conservation strategy

steps.title: Analyze biodiversity loss
steps.content: The data provided shows a drastic decline in species richness from $125$ to $45$ species over $10$ years due to deforestation. To stop this trend, we can use a **Legislative and Socio-economic Approach**, which focuses on laws and financial incentives to protect habitats.

steps.title: Legislative: International trade regulation
steps.content: One strategy is using international agreements like **CITES** (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species). This legislative tool regulates or bans the trade of endangered species and their products. By making the trade of rainforest species illegal, it reduces the economic incentive for poaching and habitat destruction, helping to maintain biodiversity levels.

steps.title: Socio-economic: Sustainable land-use
steps.content: Another strategy is implementing **ecotourism**. This is a socio-economic approach that provides local communities with a sustainable source of income that depends on a healthy, standing forest. When the forest becomes more valuable as a tourist destination than as timber or farmland, the economic pressure for deforestation is greatly reduced.

steps.title: Summary of strategies
steps.content:
In summary, the two strategies are:
1. **Legislative:** Using international laws (like CITES) or national laws to designate protected areas/national parks to prevent legal logging.
2. **Socio-economic:** Promoting ecotourism to provide alternative livelihoods that protect rather than destroy the ecosystem.

questions.specification:
A small population of endangered Amur leopards was declining rapidly in the wild. Conservation biologists established a facility where they could safely breed these leopards under controlled conditions, successfully maintaining stable numbers of the species.

![Amur leopard](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/474d005a-9dca-44c0-8937-6488e8797558)

Which conservation strategy is being demonstrated?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.2 Conservation of biodiversity > A4.2.7 Need for several approaches to conservation of biodiversity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.2.7 Need for several approaches to conservation of biodiversity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify a conservation strategy (captive breeding) used to stabilize an endangered population, which is a specific example of the various approaches to biodiversity conservation covered in this topic.

options.content: Habitat restoration
options.markscheme: Habitat restoration involves improving or restoring the natural environment where a species lives, rather than breeding them in a controlled facility.

options.content: Genetic modification
options.markscheme: Genetic modification involves the direct manipulation of an organism's genes using biotechnology, which is not the process described here.

options.content: Captive breeding
options.markscheme: - Identify captive breeding as the process of breeding endangered species in a controlled environment, such as a zoo or specialized facility, to increase or stabilize their population. <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Biocontrol
options.markscheme: Biocontrol is the use of one biological species (e.g., a predator or parasite) to control the population of another (e.g., a pest species).

parts.content: Describe one method used to estimate species biodiversity in an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:

- Count the number of species present (species richness) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Count individuals of each species for relative abundance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Repeat sampling to ensure accuracy and reduce bias <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a diversity index (e.g. Simpson’s or Shannon index) to calculate diversity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare diversity across different areas or time periods <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Method provides a baseline for conservation efforts <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how energy is transferred through a food chain.
parts.markscheme:
- Producers capture sunlight via photosynthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Primary consumers eat producers, gaining stored chemical energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy is passed to secondary and tertiary consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Decomposers obtain energy from dead organisms and recycle nutrients <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy transfer is inefficient, with approximately $10\%$ passed between levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy is lost as heat via respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy supports growth, reproduction, and movement at each level <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.5 Differences between individuals in adaptation, survival and reproduction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic (D4.1.5) explains how variation among individuals leads to differences in adaptation and survival through natural selection, which is the biological mechanism by which genetic diversity ensures species survival.

parts.content: Explain the role of genetic diversity in species survival and conservation.
parts.markscheme:
- Genetic diversity refers to the variety of alleles or genes present in a population. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It enables populations to adapt to changing environments or selective pressures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High genetic diversity increases the likelihood that some individuals possess alleles that offer a selective advantage. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individuals with beneficial traits are more likely to survive and reproduce, passing these traits to offspring (natural selection). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetic diversity helps species resist diseases, preventing a single pathogen from wiping out the entire population. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It reduces the risk of inbreeding and the associated inbreeding depression. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It prevents or buffers against genetic drift, which can lead to the loss of beneficial alleles in small populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conservation efforts (e.g., seed banks, captive breeding) focus on maintaining genetic diversity to ensure long-term population viability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Loss of genetic diversity can lead to reduced biological fitness and increased extinction risk. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for clear examples that illustrate these points.</note>

<examTip>Link genetic diversity specifically to survival mechanisms when explaining adaptation.</examTip>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.10 Allele frequencies of geographically isolated populations (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A key importance of ecological corridors is maintaining gene flow between otherwise isolated populations, which directly relates to the study of allele frequencies in geographically isolated populations.

parts.content: Outline one way habitat fragmentation can reduce genetic diversity in a population.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced movement between habitat patches limits gene flow, increasing genetic drift and loss of alleles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smaller, isolated populations are more likely to inbreed, increasing homozygosity and reducing genetic variation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bottlenecks/founder effects may occur when only a few individuals remain or colonise patches, reducing allele diversity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced effective population size makes random changes in allele frequency more significant, accelerating allele loss <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one management strategy (other than corridors) that could help conserve biodiversity in fragmented landscapes.
parts.markscheme:
- Increase size of protected areas/reserves to support larger populations and reduce edge effects <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Create stepping-stone habitats to allow gradual dispersal between patches <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Restore degraded habitat to increase habitat quality and carrying capacity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implement buffer zones around fragments to reduce human impacts (pollution, invasive species, disturbance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Control invasive species/predators to reduce additional pressure on small isolated populations <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the importance of ecological corridors in maintaining biodiversity in fragmented landscapes.
parts.markscheme:
- Ecological corridors provide connectivity between isolated/fragmented habitats <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allow movement and dispersal of species between habitat patches <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase gene flow, helping maintain genetic diversity within populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced genetic isolation lowers inbreeding and inbreeding depression <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitate recolonisation/rescue effect after local extinctions, increasing metapopulation persistence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enable seasonal/annual migration and access to resources (feeding/breeding sites), supporting wider-ranging species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase population and ecosystem resilience to environmental change/disturbance by allowing range shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for any other relevant points related to biodiversity maintenance through ecological corridors</note>
